title: The BrightPath Outage: Students Locked Out

personality.role: junior SRE, first solo on-call shift

facts: professor tweeted about BrightPath being down during midterm week
engineering lead just joined the call
last deployment was a security hardening sprint
they tightened firewall rules across all environments
diagnosis does not match observed symptoms
problem appears to be network access
check what traffic is allowed to reach the instance

glossary.Security Group: A virtual firewall that controls inbound and outbound traffic for an EC2 instance. It works at the instance level and only has allow rules -- if traffic is not explicitly allowed, it is silently dropped.
glossary.Inbound Rule: A security group rule that defines what incoming traffic is permitted to reach the instance. Each rule specifies a protocol, port range, and source (such as an IP range or another security group).
glossary.CIDR Block: A notation like 0.0.0.0/0 that represents a range of IP addresses. 0.0.0.0/0 means all IPv4 addresses on the internet.
glossary.Port 443: The standard network port for HTTPS traffic. Web browsers use this port when connecting to sites over a secure (TLS/SSL) connection.
glossary.Default Deny: The behavior where all traffic is blocked unless a rule explicitly allows it. Security groups use default deny for inbound traffic -- no rule means no access.
glossary.Network ACL: A stateless firewall that operates at the subnet level, one layer above the security group. It has both allow and deny rules and evaluates them in order by rule number.
glossary.Stateful Firewall: A firewall that remembers active connections. If an inbound rule allows a request in, the response is automatically allowed out without needing a separate outbound rule.
glossary.VPC: Virtual Private Cloud -- an isolated virtual network in AWS where you launch resources like EC2 instances. It has its own IP address range, subnets, and routing.
glossary.Internet Gateway: A VPC component that allows communication between instances in a public subnet and the internet. Without it, no internet traffic can reach the VPC.
glossary.Health Check: An automated test that verifies whether an application or instance is responding correctly. A failing health check does not always mean the instance is broken -- it could mean traffic cannot reach it.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening
narrative_arc.threshold: player enters investigation, instance running but unreachable externally, usual explanations ruled out
narrative_arc.trials: instance healthy, app running, metrics normal, traffic drops to zero at 19:42 UTC -- problem invisible from inside
narrative_arc.revelation: security group has no inbound rule for port 443, removed during hardening sprint
narrative_arc.return: one rule added, traffic restores in seconds, post-incident review

components.name: Internet Gateway (igw-0f1e2d3c4b5a)
components.role: Routes inbound HTTPS traffic from students on the public internet into the VPC
components.failure_impact: If removed or detached, no internet traffic reaches the VPC at all

components.name: Public Subnet (10.0.1.0/24)
components.role: Hosts the EC2 web server in an internet-accessible subnet within the VPC
components.failure_impact: If route table lacks a default route to the internet gateway, outbound and inbound internet traffic stops

components.name: Network ACL (acl-0fedcba987654321)
components.role: Stateless subnet-level firewall that evaluates inbound and outbound rules by rule number
components.failure_impact: If a deny rule for port 443 is added above the allow rule, traffic is blocked at the subnet boundary

components.name: Security Group (sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890)
components.role: Stateful instance-level firewall that controls which traffic can reach the EC2 instance
components.failure_impact: If the inbound rule for port 443 is missing, all HTTPS traffic is silently dropped before reaching the application

components.name: EC2 Instance (brightpath-prod-web-01)
components.role: Runs the BrightPath LMS application on port 443, serving student traffic
components.failure_impact: If the instance stops or the application crashes, health checks fail and no requests are served

components.name: RDS MySQL (brightpath-db)
components.role: Stores course materials, student submissions, and user accounts
components.failure_impact: If the database is unreachable, the application returns errors but the site still loads partially

system_narration.data_flow: Students send HTTPS requests to the public IP 54.210.128.33. Traffic enters the VPC through the Internet Gateway, passes through the Network ACL at the subnet boundary, then through the Security Group attached to the EC2 instance. The application on port 443 processes the request, queries RDS over the private subnet, and returns the response back through the same path.
system_narration.what_broke: At 19:42 UTC, the inbound rule allowing TCP 443 from 0.0.0.0/0 was removed from security group sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 during a security hardening sprint. The security group reverted to default-deny for HTTPS. Every student request was silently dropped at the security group boundary. The instance, application, and database continued running normally -- the break was purely at the network access layer.

hints.hint: application is running on the instance but nothing reaches it from outside -- what network rules control access?
security groups act as virtual firewalls for EC2 instances -- what inbound rules are configured?
check inbound rules for port 443 -- the application serves HTTPS traffic on that port
security group has no inbound rule for port 443 -- all HTTPS traffic from users is being dropped at the network level

resolution.root_cause: The EC2 instance security group sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 was modified during a security hardening sprint, removing the inbound rule for port 443 (HTTPS). All user traffic to the BrightPath learning platform is dropped at the security group level.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the security group is missing an inbound rule for port 443 (HTTPS)
Player connects the missing rule to the recent security hardening deployment
Player proposes adding an inbound rule allowing TCP port 443 from 0.0.0.0/0 (or appropriate CIDR) to the security group

learning_objectives: Security groups are stateful firewalls that control inbound and outbound traffic to EC2 instances
Security group rules are deny-by-default -- if no rule explicitly allows traffic, it is dropped
Changes to security groups take effect immediately with no downtime or restart required
sop_steps: 1. Acknowledge the alert and join the incident channel so the team knows someone is investigating.

2. Check whether the server is actually running. Use the describe-instances command to confirm the instance brightpath-prod-web-01 shows a 'running' state. A running instance means the machine itself is on -- it does not mean users can reach it.

3. Look at the application logs in CloudWatch (a monitoring service that collects logs and metrics). Confirm the app is healthy internally but is not receiving any incoming traffic -- this points to a network-level block rather than an app crash.

4. Check the NetworkPacketsIn metric in CloudWatch for this instance. This metric counts how many network packets arrive at the server. If it dropped to zero around 19:42 UTC, something started blocking all incoming traffic at that time.

5. Inspect the security group attached to this instance. A security group is a virtual firewall that controls what traffic is allowed to reach the server. Use describe-security-groups to list all its inbound rules -- the rules that say which incoming traffic is permitted.

6. Look for a rule that allows TCP traffic on port 443 (the standard port for HTTPS, or secure web traffic) from 0.0.0.0/0 (meaning any IP address on the internet). If that rule is missing, every web request from users is being silently dropped.

7. Also check the Network ACL -- a second layer of firewall that operates at the subnet level (a subnet is a section of your virtual network). Confirm it allows port 443 inbound, so you can rule it out and focus on the security group as the cause.

8. Fix the problem by adding an inbound rule to the security group: allow TCP traffic on port 443 from 0.0.0.0/0 (for IPv4 users) and ::/0 (for IPv6 users). Security group changes take effect instantly -- no server restart needed.

9. Verify the fix works by checking that the /health endpoint (a URL that reports whether the app is online) responds from an external network. Also confirm the CloudWatch health check alarm goes back to OK.
10. Write up what happened, when it happened, and how it was fixed. Post it in the incident review channel so the team can learn from it and prevent similar issues.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Network ACL deny rule blocks port 443
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of the security group (the firewall around the server), the problem is in the Network ACL -- a separate firewall that protects the entire subnet (a section of your network). Network ACLs evaluate rules in numbered order, and a deny rule with a lower number than an allow rule wins. Unlike security groups, Network ACLs are stateless, meaning you need separate rules for traffic going in and coming out. The security group would look fine in this case, making it harder to find.
related_failure_modes.prevention: After any changes, review Network ACL rules to make sure allow rules for required ports have lower rule numbers than any broad deny rules. Turn on VPC Flow Logs -- a feature that records which traffic was accepted or rejected -- to see exactly where traffic is being blocked.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Route table missing default route to Internet Gateway
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Every subnet has a route table that tells traffic where to go. If the route table loses its default route (the 0.0.0.0/0 entry that sends internet-bound traffic to the Internet Gateway), all internet traffic stops reaching the subnet -- not just one port. The server also loses its ability to reach the internet, which is different from a security group issue where only inbound traffic is affected.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Label production route tables clearly and use AWS Config rules (automated compliance checks) to detect when a public subnet's route table is missing its default route to the Internet Gateway.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Elastic IP or public IP disassociated from instance
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Every server that needs to be reached from the internet must have a public IP address. If the public IP gets removed, the server becomes unreachable on all ports -- not just one. You can spot this by checking the instance details: the public IP field will be empty.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Use an Elastic IP (a permanent public IP address that you own) for production servers instead of auto-assigned public IPs that can change. Set up a CloudWatch alarm to alert if a production server loses its public IP.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Security group connection tracking limit exceeded
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The firewall rules are correct, but the server is handling so many simultaneous connections that it hits a built-in limit on how many connections the security group can track. This causes intermittent timeouts under heavy load rather than a complete outage. You can spot this by checking ENA driver metrics (network card statistics) for a counter called conntrack_allowance_exceeded going up.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Monitor the connection tracking metrics from the network card. If you are hitting limits, move to a larger server type that supports more connections, or adjust security group rules to reduce tracking overhead.

sop_practices: Before and after changing any firewall rules, check them against a list of ports your application needs to be reachable on. Automate this check in your deployment pipeline (CI/CD) so human error cannot skip it.

Test firewall changes in a staging environment (a copy of your production setup used for testing) before applying them to the real production servers. The staging network layout should match production so the test is meaningful.

Turn on VPC Flow Logs for your production subnets. Flow Logs record every connection attempt and whether it was accepted or rejected. When something goes wrong, these logs let you quickly see where traffic is being blocked.

Set up CloudWatch alarms that check whether your application is reachable from outside your network -- not just whether the server is running. Instance status checks only confirm the machine is on; they do not catch firewall misconfigurations that block user traffic.
